title: Fellow Gen 2 Ode Brew Grinder - Burr, Electric Coffee Bean Grinder with 31 Settings for Drip, French Press & Cold Brew - Small Footprint - Matte Black
description:
About this item
MEET THE NEW BURRS - With a unique two-stage grinding geometry, Gen 2 Brew Burrs produce a sweet, balanced, complex cup with enough clarity to respect the coffee. Whether you’re a light roast drinker looking to increase extraction yield, or a medium and dark roast lover seeking rich chocolate and toffee notes, these burrs have something for everyone.
PRECISION GRIND QUALITY - Precise particle size is possible with every push of the button thanks to cafe-sized flat burrs and easy grind adjustment with 11 settings and 31 steps total.
MESS FREE MORNINGS - With new anti-static technology, a magnetically aligned catch for added cleanliness, and a grinds knocker to reduce grind retention, Ode Gen 2 will mitigate mess and keep your brewing area tidy.
QUIET GRIND NOISE - No more waking up your roommates. From high-quality materials to ditching the noisy gearbox, Ode was designed with quietness in mind.
FELLOW - Experts in coffee with a love for product design, we at Fellow believe coffee is a journey. We are dedicated to melding beautiful design with flawless functionality to help aficionados and beginners alike find the fun in the brewing process.
reviews_5s:
I have owned this grinder over a year now. I use it for drip coffee and cold brew, it does an amazing job and is a key "ingredient" to great coffee. Its noise level is not too loud or an offensive pitch when grinding in the morning. It does not make a mess, and has been totally reliable and trouble free. The cup comes with 'fins' for some reason, they seem to get in the way, so I removed them by bending them until they let go.When I pulled the trigger on a Moccamaster Select coffee maker, I decided to upgrade my grinder at the same time. The Ode grinders frequently show up in articles and videos about that coffee maker due to its grind consistency for filter coffee and its price point. I've been using both for a month now and I've been very impressed.

Like all Fellow products, the Ode has a simple and elegant design that looks great on the counter. The build quality is solid and the grind size dial turns easily and has a nice feel. The bean hopper was adjusted for the Gen 2 grinder and is both deeper and steeper than the Gen 1, so you can grind more beans at once and they easily slide down into the burrs without getting stuck up top.

As far as grinders go, the Ode is surprisingly quiet. I worry about the noise of grinding coffee first thing in the morning, but it's hardly noticeable from other rooms even with open doors. The burrs are widely regarded as being some of the best for general filter coffee grinding, too, and produce consistent grinds every time.

The other main concern for me is retention and mess. Fellow seems to have put a lot of effort in this area for the Gen 2. Bean/grind retention is negligible, meaning you get out nearly everything that you put in with minimal leftovers. This is good both from a waste perspective and from a future-grind perspective (no stale beans hanging out in the grinder during your next use).

Their "mess-free mornings" bullet point isn't just marketing speak, either. The magnetic catch cup slides into place nicely and their anti-static tech on the output chute is genius -- it works brilliantly and really does make for almost no mess during the grind process.

As long as you're not looking to grind for espresso, you probably aren't going to find a better grinder at this price point.Holy cow. Five stars aren't nearly enough. Absolutely incredible from every perspective.

Over the years I've had four different grinders. All left the same impression: they're loud and require lots and lots of clean-up.

Not this one! You will be shocked at just how quiet it is in action. Really, really, really quiet. You'll be even more crazy over how little mess it generates. It seems to defy and downright violate whatever scientific laws exist regarding static generation. And it works quickly! And the grind quality is remarkably consistent! Where have you been my whole life? What did I do to deserve you?

The hype is real. This thing is incredible. Buy and be happy.I got this grinder for $276 on a black Friday sale. I was hesitant to spend that much money on a grinder but I knew it wasn't going to get any cheaper. I love the compact height and the hefty weight. It is very quiet and churns though my coffee beans effortlessly. The ionizer works great, I thought for sure I would need to spray but it does just fine with dry beans. I love how simple it is. Just a dial and a button, the way it should be. I am excited to use it in the mornings.

As for taste, well I have very little to compare it to but it seems just fine. My previous grinder was a Krups electric burr model which I had for years. It produced a ton of fines and lots of static. I disliked that Krups from the day I first bought it.
This machine completely outclasses that cheap grinder so a taste comparison is useless.UPDATE: Still really liking this grinder. After about 6 weeks I started having serious static problems. Coffee grounds were sticking everywhere! It turns out you need to clean the grinder about every 60 cycles to keep the anti-static tech happy. For me using it for two cups a day that would be every 30 days (or until the static becomes unbearable). This information is not in the manual but Fellow has a short video about it on their youtube channel. It's quick and easy to do. No taking anything apart, just use the provided cleaning brush to clear out any accumulated coffee in the exit chute that may be obstructing the anti-static pins. Works a charm!

ORIGINAL REVIEW: I've had the Ode Gen 2 for about a week now, using it for Aeropress cups twice a day. It replaced a $200 Breville Smart Grinder Pro which had a decently long run before failing. The $345 Ode is a step up from the Breville in almost every way. It can't do Espresso like the Breville can, but that wasn't important to me.

The Ode is much faster and quieter than the Breville. I haven't specifically timed the Ode, but to grind 17 grams of beans it's certainly less than 10 seconds between pressing the start button and the motor coming to a complete stop after detecting no more beans in the hopper. The Breville's timed dose took 19.4 seconds to grind the same quantity.

I really like the single dose grinding of the Ode. It's one of the reasons I decided to upgrade rather than buy another Breville. When a visitor was here, changing beans in the Breville was tedious. No problem with the Ode since it doesn't store any beans.

The grind produced by the Ode is extremely consistent. Every Aeropress cup I've made with the Ode has been full and flavorful with no sour notes. Aeropress with the Breville was usually very good as well, but there would be the occasional cup that was slightly off. Haven't had that happen with the Ode yet.

Of course, the styling. The Ode is quite a bit smaller than the Breville, fitting under the cabinets with more room to spare. And, as my girlfriend said the first time she saw the Ode on the counter, "It's cute!"

What do I not like about the Ode? Despite the anti-static technology in this version, I do get some stray grounds on the countertop. It's just a small amount, a few grounds here and there. Maybe it happens when I forget to tap the knocker on the side? Or maybe when I *don't* forget? Not sure yet. It's not a lot of stray grounds, and much better than some older grinders I used. The Breville on the other hand was a lot cleaner: almost never any stray grounds at all.

I also think the catch cup still needs some work. The cup itself is a thing of beauty, but the lid is finnicky, and maybe that's why I sometimes get stray grounds. The channel inside the cup to direct the beans or grounds is kinda ok but seems more trouble than it's worth. To be fair, I also didn't care for the lid on the Breville catch cup. It screwed on tight, but the rubber thingy covering the hole just never fit quite right.

The main thing I can't compare is durability since I've only had the Ode for a week. My Breville lasted about 7 years, but reviews are indicating that the latest models are failing much more quickly, which is the main reason I decided to go with a different grinder.

Overall, I'm very happy with the Ode for my Aeropress cups.I had a conical grinder I got here for less than $100 before this that was doing a good job, a great one in fact. I’ve been really trying to up my pour over method quality. I own pretty much all the methods (siphon, V60, Chemex, aeropress and derived), and while I’m getting great results I still feel like I’m a bit behind the high end coffee places I visit frequently. Upon talking to many baristas, they all agree that the grinder plays a huge role. So after 1.5 years on the fence, I pulled the trigger on this.

First off, it’s beautiful. It works super well. It’s clean (no need to spray your beans before grinding) and the cheat sheet they provide for grinding under the lid is pretty spot on. So that already makes it an upgrade.

The burs are very consistent. I can already see that the grinds are very homogenous. No thick spots around.

Flavor is a lot more pronounced. A lot sweeter and this is a common thing you hear about this grinder. While I consider the previous one I was using did an amazing job, this is probably the best upgrade I’ve done.

I consider this a great upgrade if you are looking to get pro results in the comfort of your home. This is a must have if you are very serious about your coffee game since receiving the machine I’ve already done a variety of methods and I can definitely tell that there’s noticeable differences and how good the extraction I’m getting is.

Like with a lot of things, I have to admit that the law of diminishing returns still plays out here. My previous conical bur grinder while inconsistent, still yielded great results at around 30% the cost of this machine. But if you are very serious about your coffee and it’s flavor, then you might as well take the hit. Only negative side to this machine is that it will not grind for espresso. So if you are considering this to grind for your espresso machine at home, this is the wrong grinder. Definitely the Rolls-Royce of poor over coffee grinders.Had this for almost a year. Use it religiously. Almost never take it apart to clean it. I’ve only had to disassemble it twice, once to clear a jammed bean and once to clear some compacted grounds causing the grinder to spin off balance.

This thing was designed to be maintained by its owner. Disassembly and reassembly is stupid easy. Fixed it each time with just a screw driver.

This is my favorite thing in my kitchen. I had to come back and give 5 stars… I love a properly designed thing that does what it’s advertised to do and is built to last.

Oh and the coffee tastes delicious. I use the XF pour over filters.Wow, how I love this new machine. Nearly everything I've ever heard before this one is loud and I'd never turn it on without having my noise canceling earbuds in place first. This thing is quiet. It's very clean as the deionizer works superbly. It shuts off automatically after the beans have all been ground. The hopper is 100g capacity, I think Some might call it a single dose machine. I consider this as being a single brew grinder. It'll hold all the beans that I need to make a pot of coffee or a cup, but not more. Which is great. Keep your beans sealed and fresh for the next brew.I purchased this around 6 months ago. My former grinder, a KitchenAid Pro Coffee Mill, had been with me for around 20 years. I was never really happy with the coffee I had. I kept trying different brewers and different coffees and still wasn't completely happy. Then several years ago I read and article where it said the grind consistency was the most important thing in good coffee making. Lots of dust and very fine grind mixed with the regular grind size caused a bitterness to the brew. The fine grinds brewed faster than the regular sized grinds. This caused the fine grinds to over-brew, thus causing the burnt bitterness in the brew. Only really good grinders had a real even grind consistency. I couldn't afford to upgrade at the time, so suffered with what I had. About 8 months ago I bought a new coffee brewer to replace my siphon brewer, which because of my age I was getting tired of messing the the high maintenance they required. I bought a Bunn for convenience and because had the right water temperature. It done a good job, but still had that slight bitterness in the cup. I finally broke down and saved a couple of months the bought this Fellow Gen 2 Ode Brew Grinder, in part because of a pro review I found online. The first pot of coffee I made was quiet good, but I thought I might have the grind set a bit low for two fine a grind. I moved it from the 8 setting to the 9 setting (which is coarser) and brewed another pot. Excellent brew! This was what I have been after for a very long time now! I'm very glad I took the chance and bought this grinder. I am now a firm believer of the grinder being the most important thing in great coffee making!
Dave LeonardI am astounded at how solid this grinder is and how quiet it is. I only grind for French press, but it works very well. Fast, quiet, sturdy.
reviews_1s:
UPDATE: 12Dec23: I've only had this grinder a few months now. My first one turned itself on and off randomly, even in the middle of the night. I had it replaced and imagined all was well. This morning, the grinder jammed. In any other grinder I have owned, clearing a jam isn't a big deal. I watched a video Fellows made on cleaning and unjamming the burrs and thought, that seems simple. WRONG. I removed the 4 larger screws and a small bar that is lodged in the rotating shaft. It's supposed to just pop out now. WRONG. I finally managed to dislodge it and pull it out. Btw. There were coffee grounds everywhere, behind the cover piled and jammed, showing terrible original tolerances and fit. I cleaned everything and reassembled it. Oh, btw, they whimsically show a plastic toy dinosaur lodged in their burrs. Hilarious. I turned it on and it is 3 times louder now! It is totally out of alignment. It doesn't shut itself off with no beans present and when i put beans in to test it, it jammed again immediately, even though I had it set to the coarsest setting. I have already wasted an hour on this piece of junk. Disassembled, cleaned and reassembled it 3 times. Festively, I see metallic flecks in the grind so the burrs are grinding against each other. I wont have to buy glitter for the holidays. Totally unusable and dangerous. I will dive back in but feel certain the only solution will be to pack it up and send it in to Fellows for service. More wasted time and time without a grinder and probably at my own postage expense. Please, consumers, yes it looks hip and pretty, but do yourself a big favor and DO NOT BUY THIS HORRIBLE MACHINE. I will now have either a $350 paper weight or a machine that will never work correctly again, Shame on you Fellows. As a first step, make a proper cleaning video for the consumers you have scalped who only want some morning coffee.

ORIGINAL REVIEW: I spent many hours doing research and watching videos and reviews of grinders in this price range. Many seemed to have similar sentiments regarding this ODE 2. That is, it works relatively well, beautiful design but some cheap feeling details. I appreciated Fellows addressing the universal consensus of weaknesses in the first version, namely the need for improved burrs, a too shallow bin, and static cling making a mess. I agree with the consensus that the bin lid still feels somewhat cheap. It should be more solid feeling and better fitting and a simple magnet to hold it securely could easily remedy that. Same with that rubberized lid over the retaining cup. The cup itself is substantial and snaps in place via a magnet. But that lid on the cup just isnt quality or secure. Not unattractive, but it just doesn't fit securely with any authority. Many mention the fins inside the cup that retain grounds and so requires elaborate tapping and pounding the cup to fully empty it.. I overcame that by simply emptying the cup on the other side avoiding contact with the fins. But still it's just thoughtless design easily fixed at really no cost if they so desire. All those things are disappointing since they could have been easily fixed but not my big eyebrow raise. As soon as I unboxed and plugged the grinder in, any small bump or touch anywhere on the body made it turn on. Weird. But more strange is that even unattended it turns itself on! I woke up in the middle of the night to the sound of my new grinder turning grinding nothing! Other times I put my beans in and it won't turn off but acts like it's trying to purge something stuck in there. I opened up the adjustment coarser, tap it, turn it upside down but nothing is inside. I've since discovered that unplugging it and plugging it back in seems to reset everything and it functions like nothing was ever wrong. That little push button on the front also seems a bit cheap to the touch and honestly would be better positioned on the side. Sitting so low on the front just seems not in line with it's otherwise well thought out design. Oh yes, flicking that latch on the side meant to knock loose any grounds trapped in the burrs also turns the grinder back on. I've only had it about a week and am considering sending it back for an exchange, or just looking at other options. It's a real shame since I really like the grinder, it's compact look, the automatic shutoff, when it functions properly and the fairly consistent grind. As one popular online reviewer expressed, they raised the price $50 and it's a bit iffy if it feels like a machine at the present price point, especially with many new contenders and competitors. From my current experience so far, I can't recommend this grinder,with some of the build quality and it's erratic behavior. As much as I want to love this machine, it's been disappointing. As if to reinforce my frustration, as I'm writing this moment, it just turned itself on, grinding air. I also wrote to Fellow for suggestions but a week later have received no response.I decided after 20 years ro upgrade my $25 Krups coffee mill and was taken in by the slick marketing hype of Fellow. The Ode 2 is a lovely piece of equipment and pretty quiet. It does a nice job grinding coffee. However, imagine my surprise when after a week it started making weird noises and then froze up and beeped a couple of times. I took it apart per the instructions for cleaning the burr grinders (which should not need to be done more than every month or so). The hole through which the ground coffee drops out into the container was completely jammed up with ground coffee. So I cleaned it all thoroughly and put it back together and was able to grind coffee for my breakfast cup.

Sure enough, the following week it starts making the bad noises again and freezes up. Same drill, take it apart and clean it. I shot off a note to Fellow customer service thinking there might be something wrong with my machine. To my surprise, the answer was just a series of maintenance suggestions -- clean it more often!

It is clear to me that it is simply badly designed and the clogging is a "feature" not a "bug" as the tech guys say. I am trying to get Fellow to give me my money back and so far have gotten nowhere. Save yourself a lifetime of cleaning your extremely expensive coffee grinder every few days and buy something else.

Update: After a lot of back and forth, Fellow had me send it bacxk to them and sent me a new one. Guess what? It also jammed after a few uses and has exactly the same problems. This rather clearly demonstrates that the Ode 2 has a fundamental design defect. The chute that the ground coffee drops through is either too small, shaped incorrectly, or in the wrong place.

Now I am caught in a death loop between Fellow and Amazon trying to get my money back. Fellow sent me a return label and I sent the second unit back also. But they won't give me a refund, claiming it has to come from Amazon. Of course Amazon says it is a third party seller and the refund has to come from Fellow. My next step is to file a merchant dispute with my cedit card issuer. I suspect that will be effective in getting my money back especially since I don't even have the grinder any more.

DO NOT BUY AN ODE 2!!!!!!Researched ton, bought after cusinart grinder failed 1.3 years of no issue till it failed use. Thought this is my opportunity to buy a much heralded and more expensive grinder. Time to experience and enjoy the expensive burrs. Ode 2 worked great and as expected for 30 days. 31st day filled hopper first use of day got about 1/3 through it sputtered and stopped grinding and beep beep. I normally grind 2 to 3 breville scooper scoops a day only. I cleaned it out top and bottom and put 2 scoops and less than half of one scoop sputtered and stop grinding and beep beep. The ode 2 turns on but does not spin anymore. With Amazons help I sent several messages via amazon app to fellows and no response from fellows. During this time I contacted cusiniart and they sent me a replacement grinder no questions asked 1.5 months into 18 month warranty. The point a $50 dollar grinder got replaced yet a $350 grinder no response. To be fair it was through amazon message to fellows but fellows did not respond. The cusiniart grinder never had any issues and just grinded until one day it just would not turn on. The cusiniart was a workhorse. Tinkered with it but could not get it to work. Anyway it is being replaced under warranty. I would say stay away from ode 2 until it gets worked out and also the fellows customer service is highly suspect. So while I have the cusiniart ill be on the hunt for another more expensive grinder that will back up its warranty.I suggest staying away from this grinder. While it looks great, I’ve had numerous problems and Fellow has been unresponsive to my requests for support. First, the grinder jams constantly. Even with only a few beans in the hopper, it will jam and I have to disassemble it and clean it out. Second, the static issue causes grinds to go all over and cake along the rim. Third, I read in other threads to try using fewer beans or switching to light roasts to prevent jams. If I’m paying for a quality grinder, I should not have to modify my use of the grinder for it to work properly. And sorry, but I should be able to use whatever roast I want. Fourth, the hopper does not feed beans into the grinder smoothly. If I pour in a handful of beans, they just sit in the hopper and I have to hand feed them down. It’s more annoying than a real issue. Lastly, Fellow has been unresponsive to support requests. I expected better since I’ve purchased other products such as their vacuum canisters, French press, and kettle. The grinder is a big miss on their part and does not operate as advertised.I have followed the online instructions for taking off the front burr, as you can see in photos, the 4 screws are off, but the dial (which contains the front burr) will not come off. I reached out to fellow support about this, and have been given the round about, and no support or warranty replacement. They even suggested "After consulting with our warranty team, we regret to inform you that we are unable to replace your Ode due to the screw issue. However, we have heard from some customers that a quick Google search on how to remove a screw can be helpful." Even thought I provided them the same images and explanation. Issue has nothing to do with screws.

Oh, the reason for me trying to remove the front burr is to try to clean it, because it is chirping. (Yes, I've calibrated the dial too). Guessing the burrs them self are damaged from factory.

I wish I never bought this grinder. Save your $380 and spend it else where!
It also does produce too many fines like others have reported.I paid over $300 for this and it broke after 4 uses. You can hear the burrs chattering in the video. Returning this costs time I'll never get back.This grinder is annoying. The beans jam in the feed hopper and must be poked to continue grinding. If I try to grind, let's say, 40 grams I have to poke the beans at least three times to move them along while grinding. The outlet gets coated in grounds soon after being cleaned. This causes the grounds to scatter all around the catch cup. I guess there is supposed to be some sort of deionizer at the outlet but I don't see where it does anything. After a few days the outlet is caked with grounds. I am not using dark roasted oily beans either. Just generic whole beans from Costco. This is so very annoying as I had heard that this grinder made little to no mess. That is not true at all. I should have returned this thing, but I waited too long.This thing would be useful as a paperweight and that's it. It jams everytime I use it. Sometimes I can clear the jam by slapping and shaking the machine while turning the coarseness adjustment dial to maximum. And some days I have to take the whole thing apart and clear it and then reassemble it. When I wake up in the morning and simply want my coffee, this is making me very angry. On top of that I can't return it to Amazon because the 30 day window elapsed (I've only had this piece of junk for 60 days).I ordered this grinder after doing loads of research and watching professional reviews. Right out of the box I was going to make a batch of cold brew so I plugged it in and tossed some beans in and less then one grind cycle in it jammed and it played its “SOS” signal.

I folllowed the tips in the manual to unclog/unjam but it still wouldn’t continue grinding. Pretty disappointed in what I was hopeful would be an excellent product. Back it goes.For the price, I expect a whole lot better - soon after it arrived, it started jamming up. It would get stuck and then stopped grinding. And now, just a little over a year after owning this, it's not even useable.
